data_sources: National Bureau of Statistics of China

stale_sources: Atlanta Fed GDPNow latest visible estimate dated 2026-04-09
World Gold Council ETF flow commentary published 2026-04-08 for March 2026 flows
fallbacks_used: TradingEconomics copper page used because Investing.com was blocked by policy restrictions

macro_environment.summary: Macro conditions are mixed. Equity breadth is helped by falling rate volatility and a strong semiconductor complex, but credit spreads are no longer improving and growth evidence is only modest. FX conditions favor non-USD currencies selectively, with the Australian dollar and Japanese yen showing the strongest support, while commodities are split between a bullish crude setup and neutral precious-metals signals.
key_drivers: Fed pricing remains a neutral hold, limiting directional impulse from policy
VIX remains in a balanced regime while its daily direction is lower, easing near-term equity stress
SOX is rising strongly and MOVE is falling, which is supportive for Nasdaq leadership
High-yield spreads widened slightly, tempering the broader risk-on message
Crude support remains firm through a fresh EIA inventory draw and ongoing Middle East supply disruption
Gold and silver lost a key tailwind because March 2026 gold ETF flows were record outflows

fed_stance.description: FedWatch showed 0.0% ease, 99.5% no change, and 0.5% hike for the 30 Apr 2026 meeting

real_yields.description: Flat under the ±5 bps methodology threshold; DFII10 rose from 1.90 to 1.93

usd_dxy.description: Flat on the day; DXY was up only 0.05%

vix.description: Balanced risk mood in the 15-20 range, with VIX slightly lower on the day

growth_gdpnow.description: Stable/soft growth signal; official GDPNow estimate visible on the page was 1.3%

sox_index.description: Rising strongly on the day, with additional 5-day and 1-month strength

move_index.description: Falling on the day and also lower over 5 days and 1 month

copper.description: Rising on the fallback source, with copper up 0.17% on the day

oil_inventories.description: Draw in commercial crude inventories excluding SPR from 464.717 to 463.804 million barrels

oil_supply.description: Reuters reported over 500 million barrels lost since the Iran war began and a slow restoration path despite Hormuz reopening

gold_etf_flows.description: World Gold Council reported US$12bn of March outflows, the largest monthly outflow on record

geopolitical_risk.description: Conflict effects remain material for energy supply, but the Strait of Hormuz was reported open again

ecb_stance.description: ECB kept rates unchanged on 19 March 2026 while emphasizing upside inflation risks

rba_stance.description: RBA raised the cash rate by 25 bps to 4.10% on 17 March 2026

boj_stance.description: BoJ kept the call rate around 0.75% and explicitly said it would continue raising rates if the outlook is realized

china_pmi.description: China manufacturing PMI rose from 49.0 to 50.4 in March, indicating recovery
china_pmi.source: National Bureau of Statistics of China

instruments.name: Gold
instruments.rationale: Gold is caught between offsetting forces. Real yields are flat rather than falling, the dollar is flat, and VIX is balanced, so the normal macro tailwinds are limited. Ongoing oil-supply disruption offers some inflation-hedge support, but record March ETF outflows remove a major positive driver.
risks: A renewed drop in real yields would quickly turn gold constructive
Further ETF liquidation would keep spot rallies from extending

instruments.name: Silver
instruments.rationale: Silver also sits in a cross-current. Copper is rising and supports the industrial side of silver demand, but March gold ETF outflows weaken the precious-metals complex. With flat real yields, a flat dollar, and only soft growth evidence, the net setup is balanced.
risks: Silver can outperform quickly if growth proxies strengthen further
Precious-metals weakness could dominate if ETF selling persists

instruments.name: WTI Crude Oil
instruments.rationale: Crude has the clearest positive setup in the commodity group. The latest EIA report showed a fresh commercial crude draw, while Reuters confirmed that substantial Middle East supply losses remain unresolved even after the Strait of Hormuz reopened. Flat dollar and soft-but-not-collapsing growth conditions leave the supply story dominant.
risks: A faster normalization of Gulf supply would reduce the risk premium
A sharper downturn in growth expectations would weaken the demand side

instruments.name: S&P 500
instruments.rationale: The S&P 500 sees a fairly even macro mix. Falling VIX direction is supportive, but high-yield spreads widened slightly and growth evidence remains only modest. With the Fed neutral, real yields flat, and the dollar flat, there is not enough macro asymmetry for a stronger directional call.
risks: If credit spreads continue to widen, index-level breadth could deteriorate quickly
A lower VIX without spread stress would improve the equity backdrop

instruments.name: NASDAQ 100
instruments.rationale: Nasdaq has the cleanest equity support among the index contracts. SOX is strongly higher and MOVE is falling, reducing a key headwind for duration-sensitive growth stocks. The broader macro backdrop is not aggressively risk-on, but these two sector-specific drivers are enough to keep bias modestly positive.
risks: A rebound in rate volatility would hurt the tech leadership trade
If semiconductors lose momentum, NQ's relative edge would narrow

instruments.name: Dow Jones
instruments.rationale: The Dow benefits from a steeper curve, which typically favors value and cyclicals, but that help is offset by slightly wider credit spreads and a generally neutral core macro backdrop. Without a stronger growth or credit signal, the contract remains balanced.
risks: Further curve steepening with stable credit would improve the Dow setup
A wider HY spread would hurt the cyclical/value expression

instruments.name: Russell 2000
instruments.rationale: Small caps remain the most sensitive to financing conditions, and the slight widening in high-yield spreads is therefore a meaningful drag. Curve steepening helps somewhat, but with only soft growth and no Fed easing impulse, the Russell still lacks a decisive macro catalyst.
risks: Any improvement in credit spreads would disproportionately help RTY
If growth softens further, funding-sensitive small caps could underperform again

instruments.name: Euro FX
instruments.rationale: The euro has a mild positive bias because the ECB remains on a hawkish hold while the Fed setup is simply neutral. That said, the dollar was flat rather than decisively weak on the day, and euro-area growth remains subdued. The result is a modest rather than aggressive bullish tilt.
risks: A renewed dollar upswing would quickly erase the mild euro advantage
Weak euro-area activity data would undercut the hawkish ECB support

instruments.name: Australian Dollar
instruments.rationale: The Australian dollar has the strongest FX setup in this report. The RBA has recently hiked, the rate differential versus the Fed has widened in Australia's favor, China manufacturing PMI recovered back above 50, and copper is rising. With risk mood neutral rather than risk-off, those pro-cyclical inputs dominate.
risks: AUD would lose support quickly if China data momentum fades
A sharp turn lower in copper would weaken the cyclical currency signal

instruments.name: Japanese Yen
instruments.rationale: The yen remains supported by domestic policy normalization. The BoJ's language still points toward further rate increases if the outlook holds, and that keeps the U.S.-Japan rate gap on a narrowing path. Because risk mood is only balanced and the dollar is flat, the yen's bullish case is driven mainly by rates rather than pure safe-haven demand.
risks: If U.S. rates move back up materially, the narrowing-gap thesis weakens
A broad risk-on surge could reduce demand for yen exposure
